attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with shortness of breath and is admitted for management of community-acquired pneumonia. He has a significant alcohol history, drinking approximately 15 standard drinks per day of beer and red wine over the last 10 years. His medical history includes hypertension, diabetes, and anxiety. He takes perindopril, metformin, and escitalopram.

On examination, he appears unwell with a pulse rate of 105/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, temperature 38.5°C, and SpO2 98% on room air. He has palmar erythema and spider naevi on his arms. Abdominal examination is unremarkable with no palpable masses.

He responds well to treatment for pneumonia. A decision is made to investigate his presumed alcoholic liver disease. He is found to have Child-Pugh B cirrhosis. An endoscopy reveals small oesophageal varices with no high-risk stigmata.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
options: Repeat endoscopy in one to two years
attributes.explanation:
This patient has compensated cirrhosis with small oesophageal varices without high-risk stigmata. The most appropriate management is to repeat endoscopy in one to two years. Regular endoscopic surveillance is crucial to monitor the progression of varices and prevent variceal haemorrhage. This approach allows for timely intervention if the varices enlarge or develop high-risk features.

Propranolol, a non-selective beta-blocker and current guidelines recommend its use for primary prophylaxis against variceal haemorrhage in patients with medium to large varices (≥5mm) or small varices with high-risk features in Child-Pugh C cirrhosis. High-risk stigmata include red wale marks (longitudinal red streaks on varices) and cherry-red spots (red, discrete, flat spots on varices). As this patient has small varices without high-risk features and Child-Pugh B cirrhosis, propranolol is not indicated at this time.

Liver elastography is typically used to assess liver fibrosis and aid in the diagnosis of cirrhosis. However, the presence of oesophageal varices, regardless of their size, already confirms the diagnosis of cirrhosis due to portal hypertension. Therefore, liver elastography would not provide additional useful information in this case.

No further management is an incorrect approach. Patients with cirrhosis and small varices require ongoing surveillance and management to prevent complications.

Variceal band ligation is the first-line management for active variceal bleeding. It can be used as an alternative to non-selective beta-blockade for prophylactic treatment of high-risk varices. It is not appropriate for this patient who has small varices without high-risk features. Endoscopic surveillance is preferred in this scenario to monitor for progression before considering more invasive interventions.
learningPoints: Recognise the importance of regular endoscopic surveillance for small oesophageal varices in compensated cirrhosis.
The management of non-bleeding varices is impacted by the severity of liver disease and the presence of high-risk features features
references: Angeli P, Bernardi M, Villanueva C, Francoz C, Mookerjee RP, Trebicka J, et al. EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines for the management of patients with decompensated cirrhosis. Journal of Hepatology. 2018 Aug;69(2):406–60.
Jakab SS, Garcia-Tsao G. Screening and Surveillance of Varices in Patients With Cirrhosis. Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology. 2019 Jan;17(1):26–9.

Crabb DW, Im GY, Szabo G, Mellinger JL, Lucey MR. Diagnosis and Treatment of Alcohol‐Related Liver Diseases: 2019 Practice Guidance from the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases. Hepatology [Internet]. 2019 Jul 17;71(1). Available from: https://aasldpubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/hep.30866
Mansour D, Masson S, Shawcross DL, et al. British Society of Gastroenterology Best Practice Guidance: outpatient management of cirrhosis – part 1: compensated cirrhosis. Front Gastro 2023; 14:453–61.
